---
title: eda.plots
---



`eda.plots`



## Functions

| Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| [plot_ip_boxplots](#spotoptim.eda.plots.plot_ip_boxplots) | Generate infill-point boxplots for each numerical column in a DataFrame. |
| [plot_ip_histograms](#spotoptim.eda.plots.plot_ip_histograms) | Generate infill-point histograms for each numerical column in a DataFrame. |

### plot_ip_boxplots { #spotoptim.eda.plots.plot_ip_boxplots }

```python
eda.plots.plot_ip_boxplots(
    df,
    category_column_name=None,
    num_cols=2,
    figwidth=10,
    box_width=0.2,
    both_names=True,
    height_per_subplot=2.0,
    add_points=None,
    add_points_col=['red'],
)
```

Generate infill-point boxplots for each numerical column in a DataFrame.

A separate horizontal boxplot is created for each numerical column of df
and arranged in a grid. Each subplot uses its own axis scale, mirroring the
behaviour of plot_ip_histograms. An optional categorical grouping column
splits the data into one box per category level.

If add_points is provided and its numerical columns differ from those of df
in name but agree in count, the column names of add_points are silently
replaced by those of df before plotting. This ensures that infill points
drawn from a differently named search space are always overlaid correctly.

#### Parameters {.doc-section .doc-section-parameters}

| Name                 | Type                                           | Description                                                                                                                                                            | Default    |
|----------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------|
| df                   | [pd](`pandas`).[DataFrame](`pandas.DataFrame`) | DataFrame containing the data to plot.                                                                                                                                 | _required_ |
| category_column_name | [str](`str`)                                   | Column name for categorical grouping. When provided, one box is drawn per unique category. Defaults to None.                                                           | `None`     |
| num_cols             | [int](`int`)                                   | Number of columns in the subplot grid. Defaults to 2.                                                                                                                  | `2`        |
| figwidth             | [int](`int`)                                   | Width of the entire figure in inches. Defaults to 10.                                                                                                                  | `10`       |
| box_width            | [float](`float`)                               | Width of each boxplot. Defaults to 0.2.                                                                                                                                | `0.2`      |
| both_names           | [bool](`bool`)                                 | When True, the subplot title shows both the variable name and, if applicable, the category column name. When False, only the variable name is shown. Defaults to True. | `True`     |
| height_per_subplot   | [float](`float`)                               | Height in inches allocated to each subplot row. Defaults to 2.0.                                                                                                       | `2.0`      |
| add_points           | [pd](`pandas`).[DataFrame](`pandas.DataFrame`) | DataFrame containing additional points to highlight with diamond markers. Defaults to None.                                                                            | `None`     |
| add_points_col       | [list](`list`)                                 | List of colours, one per row of add_points. Defaults to ["red"].                                                                                                       | `['red']`  |

#### Returns {.doc-section .doc-section-returns}

| Name   | Type   | Description   |
|--------|--------|---------------|
|        | None   | None          |

#### Raises {.doc-section .doc-section-raises}

| Name   | Type                       | Description                                                                      |
|--------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|        | [ValueError](`ValueError`) | If the number of rows in add_points does not equal the length of add_points_col. |

#### Examples {.doc-section .doc-section-examples}


::: {#a64042e9 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd
from spotoptim.eda.plots import plot_ip_boxplots
data = {'A': [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 100], 'B': [10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
plot_ip_boxplots(df, num_cols=1)
# Example with multiple added points and colors
add_points = pd.DataFrame({'A': [1.5, 3.5], 'B': [10, 10]})
plot_ip_boxplots(df, add_points=add_points, add_points_col=["red", "blue"])
# Example: add_points with different column names are aligned automatically
add_points_renamed = pd.DataFrame({'x': [1.5, 3.5], 'y': [10, 10]})
plot_ip_boxplots(df, add_points=add_points_renamed, add_points_col=["green", "orange"])
```

#### References {.doc-section .doc-section-references}

Bartz-Beielstein, T. (2025). Multi-Objective Optimization and Hyperparameter
Tuning With Desirability Functions. arXiv preprint arXiv:2503.23595.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.23595

### plot_ip_histograms { #spotoptim.eda.plots.plot_ip_histograms }

```python
eda.plots.plot_ip_histograms(
    df,
    bins=10,
    num_cols=2,
    figwidth=10,
    thrs_unique=5,
    add_points=None,
    add_points_col=['red'],
)
```

Generate infill-point histograms for each numerical column in a DataFrame.

A separate histogram is created for each numerical column of df and arranged
in a grid. The title of each subplot shows the total point count, the number
of unique values, the number of outliers detected via the IQR method, and
the standard deviation. Columns with fewer unique values than thrs_unique are
coloured differently to draw attention to low-variability features.

If add_points is provided and its numerical columns differ from those of df
in name but agree in count, the column names of add_points are silently
replaced by those of df before plotting. This ensures that infill points
drawn from a differently named search space are always overlaid correctly.

#### Parameters {.doc-section .doc-section-parameters}

| Name           | Type                                           | Description                                                                                                            | Default    |
|----------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------|
| df             | [pd](`pandas`).[DataFrame](`pandas.DataFrame`) | DataFrame containing the data to plot.                                                                                 | _required_ |
| bins           | [int](`int`)                                   | Number of bins for the histograms. Defaults to 10.                                                                     | `10`       |
| num_cols       | [int](`int`)                                   | Number of columns in the subplot grid. Defaults to 2.                                                                  | `2`        |
| figwidth       | [int](`int`)                                   | Width of the entire figure in inches. Defaults to 10.                                                                  | `10`       |
| thrs_unique    | [int](`int`)                                   | Threshold for unique values below which the histogram bar colour switches from lightblue to lightcoral. Defaults to 5. | `5`        |
| add_points     | [pd](`pandas`).[DataFrame](`pandas.DataFrame`) | DataFrame containing additional points to highlight with diamond markers at y = 0. Defaults to None.                   | `None`     |
| add_points_col | [list](`list`)                                 | List of colours, one per row of add_points. Defaults to ["red"].                                                       | `['red']`  |

#### Returns {.doc-section .doc-section-returns}

| Name   | Type   | Description   |
|--------|--------|---------------|
|        | None   | None          |

#### Raises {.doc-section .doc-section-raises}

| Name   | Type                       | Description                                                                      |
|--------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
|        | [ValueError](`ValueError`) | If the number of rows in add_points does not equal the length of add_points_col. |

#### Examples {.doc-section .doc-section-examples}

::: {#64b2f80d .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd
from spotoptim.eda.plots import plot_ip_histograms
data = {'A': [1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 100], 'B': [10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
plot_ip_histograms(df, bins=5, num_cols=1, thrs_unique=3)
# Example with multiple added points and colors
add_points = pd.DataFrame({'A': [1.5, 3.5], 'B': [10, 10]})
plot_ip_histograms(df, add_points=add_points, add_points_col=["red", "blue"])
# Example: add_points with different column names are aligned automatically
add_points_renamed = pd.DataFrame({'x': [1.5, 3.5], 'y': [10, 10]})
plot_ip_histograms(df, add_points=add_points_renamed, add_points_col=["green", "orange"])
```
